Remote Work Improves Productivity

Abstract. This study examines remote work and productivity for software teams.

The study finds that remote work increases productivity and improves focus for software teams. Future work should examine long-term outcomes and replication in other organizations.

Methods. The research compares distributed software teams across planning, implementation, review, and release work. Participants reported fewer interruptions, longer focus blocks, and better documentation habits when remote norms were explicit. Managers also observed that written decision records made onboarding faster and reduced repeated status meetings.

Results. Productivity gains were strongest when teams used asynchronous planning, clear ownership, and shared review queues. The study cautions that remote work does not automatically improve outcomes; teams need deliberate communication protocols, reliable tooling, and explicit expectations for response time, availability, and escalation.

Implications. Organizations adopting remote work should measure output quality, cycle time, retention, and collaboration health together. The paper recommends longitudinal replication across company sizes and product domains before treating the results as universal.
